text: Measuring how humans perceive visual content remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. While existing mathematical models can effectively quantify technical degradations like noise or blur, these metrics are often disconnected from natural language and fail to capture more abstract "feelings" associated with an image. Conventional methods for assessing such subjective qualities typically rely on supervised models trained on labor-intensive, human-annotated datasets. In this paper, we propose a shift from this paradigm by leveraging the extensive visual-language priors found in Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models. We demonstrate that CLIP can be effectively adapted to assess both the technical quality ("look") and the abstract perception ("feel") of images without the need for task-specific training. To unlock this capability, we introduce a prompt pairing strategy using antonymous adjectives to mitigate linguistic ambiguity and refine the model’s evaluative precision. Extensive experiments on standard Image Quality Assessment (IQA) benchmarks and controlled datasets indicate that our approach, CLIP-IQA, achieves a high correlation with human judgment across diverse perceptual dimensions. Our findings suggest that CLIP contains meaningful, generalized priors that can serve as a versatile foundation for zero-shot visual perception assessment.
